structured.research_question:
Does replacing sucrose with trehalose as a cryoprotectant in the freezing
medium increase post-thaw viability of HeLa cells compared to the standard
DMSO protocol?
structured.subject: HeLa cells
structured.independent:
Cryoprotectant identity (trehalose substituted for sucrose in the freezing
medium, vs. standard DMSO-based protocol).
structured.dependent:
Post-thaw cell viability, measured as percentage of viable cells after
thawing.
structured.conditions:
Standard slow-rate freezing in cryovials, storage in liquid nitrogen,
rapid thaw at 37 C, viability measured by trypan blue exclusion 24h
post-thaw. Trehalose at 0.5M, sucrose at 0.5M, DMSO at 10% v/v.
structured.expected:
Trehalose substitution increases post-thaw viability by at least 15
percentage points compared to the DMSO standard, due to trehalose's
superior membrane stabilization at low temperatures.

overall_assumptions: Standard lab equipment (cell culture hood, incubators, centrifuges, microscopes, liquid nitrogen storage) is available and calibrated.
HeLa cells are free of mycoplasma contamination.
Reagents and media are sterile and of cell culture grade.
Personnel are trained in aseptic cell culture techniques and safe handling of cryogens.
overall_controls: Positive control: Standard DMSO-based cryopreservation protocol.
Negative control: Cells cryopreserved without any cryoprotectant (expected low viability).
Sham control: Fresh, unfrozen HeLa cells measured for viability alongside thawed samples, representing 100% viability baseline.

procedures.name: HeLa Cell Culture Preparation
procedures.intent: To prepare a sufficient quantity of healthy, actively growing HeLa cells for cryopreservation.

steps.title: Retrieve and Warm Culture Medium
steps.body_md: Retrieve the complete HeLa cell culture medium (DMEM supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and 1% Penicillin/Streptomycin) from cold storage. Allow the medium to warm to room temperature (18-25°C) or place it in a 37°C water bath for approximately 30 minutes to reach recommended culture temperature.
steps.notes: Ensure the medium is completely thawed and warmed to physiological temperature before use to prevent thermal shock to the cells.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Culture medium warmed to 37°C.

steps.title: Aspirate Spent Medium
steps.body_md: Transfer the HeLa cell culture flask (e.g., T-75 flask) into a Biological Safety Cabinet (BSC). Aspirate the spent culture medium from the flask using a sterile serological pipette attached to a vacuum pump. Tilt the flask gently to ensure complete removal of medium without disturbing the cell monolayer.
steps.notes: Work aseptically at all times to prevent contamination.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Flask free of spent medium, cells adhered to surface.
troubleshooting: If cells detach during aspiration: Reduce aspiration speed and ensure the pipette tip does not directly contact the cell monolayer.

steps.title: Wash Cells with PBS
steps.body_md: Add 5-10 mL of sterile 1x Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS) without Ca2+ or Mg2+ to the flask. Gently rock the flask back and forth 2-3 times to wash the cell monolayer and remove any residual serum, which can inhibit trypsin activity. Aspirate the PBS completely.
steps.notes: Complete removal of serum is crucial for effective trypsinization.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cells washed free of residual medium and serum.

steps.title: Trypsinize Cells
steps.body_md: Add 2-3 mL of 0.25% Trypsin-EDTA solution (w/v) to the flask. Ensure the entire cell monolayer is covered. Incubate the flask at 37°C in a CO2 incubator for 3-5 minutes. Periodically check under a microscope for cell detachment. Tap the side of the flask gently if cells are slow to detach.
steps.notes: Over-trypsinization can damage cells. Under-trypsinization leads to poor cell yield. Optimize incubation time for your specific cell line and conditions.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cells appear rounded and detached from the flask surface.
troubleshooting: If cells aren't detaching: Continue incubation for another 1-2 minutes, then gently tap the flask. Avoid scraping.
If cells look granular/damaged: Reduce trypsin incubation time or concentration for future passages.

steps.title: Neutralize Trypsin and Collect Cells
steps.body_md: Once cells are detached, add 5 mL of complete HeLa cell culture medium (from Step 1) to the flask to neutralize the trypsin. Pipette the cell suspension up and down several times to dissociate cell clumps and create a single-cell suspension. Transfer the entire cell suspension to a sterile 15 mL conical tube.
steps.notes: FBS in the complete medium contains inhibitors that will neutralize trypsin.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Homogeneous single-cell suspension in a conical tube.
troubleshooting: If excessive cell clumping: Gently pipette solution more vigorously, or consider adding a small amount of DNase I to subsequent trypsinization steps if cell death is suspected.

steps.title: Centrifuge Cells
steps.body_md: Centrifuge the 15 mL conical tube containing the cell suspension at 300 x g for 5 minutes at room temperature (20-25°C).
steps.notes: Ensure the centrifuge is balanced before spinning.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A compact, visible cell pellet at the bottom of the tube.
troubleshooting: If no visible pellet: Increase centrifugation speed or duration, ensure enough cells were transferred.

steps.title: Resuspend Cells and Count
steps.body_md: Aspirate the supernatant carefully, ensuring not to disturb the cell pellet. Resuspend the cell pellet in an appropriate volume (e.g., 1-2 mL) of fresh complete HeLa cell culture medium. Gently pipette up and down to achieve a uniform single-cell suspension. Take a 10 µL aliquot and dilute 1:1 with 0.4% Trypan Blue solution. Load 10 µL of the stained cell suspension onto a hemocytometer and count viable and non-viable cells.
steps.notes: Accurate cell counting is essential for calculating seeding density and viability. Viable cells exclude trypan blue and appear bright, while non-viable cells stain blue.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cell count and viability percentage determined.
troubleshooting: If cell counts are inconsistent: Ensure proper mixing of cell suspension before taking aliquots, and clean hemocytometer thoroughly.
If viability is low (<90%): Assess health of parent culture, ensure proper trypsinization, and consider discarding the culture if viability is consistently poor.

steps.title: Adjust Cell Density for Cryopreservation
steps.body_md: Based on the cell count from Step 7, adjust the cell suspension to the required density for cryopreservation, which is typically 1 x 10^6 to 5 x 10^6 cells/mL for HeLa cells. Use complete HeLa cell culture medium to dilute the cells if necessary, or centrifuge and resuspend in a smaller volume if too dilute.
steps.notes: The exact cell density will depend on the downstream cryopreservation protocol and desired recovery.
steps.anticipated_outcome: HeLa cell suspension at the target density for cryopreservation.

procedures.name: Cryoprotectant Medium Preparation
procedures.intent: To accurately prepare the experimental cryoprotectant solutions (DMSO-based, trehalose-based, and sucrose-based) at the specified concentrations.

steps.title: Prepare DMEM + 10% FBS Base Medium (Control)
steps.body_md: Aseptically prepare urn:nil:uuid:5ca8dc4c-22ca-4c07-b2fb-ec55099fe682 of Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) containing 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) in a sterile beaker with a stir bar. This will serve as the base medium for all cryoprotectant preparations and as the control freezing medium.
steps.notes: Ensure FBS is heat-inactivated if not purchased as such, to prevent complement-mediated cell lysis.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, sterile DMEM + 10% FBS solution.

steps.title: Prepare DMSO Cryoprotectant Medium
steps.body_md: In a sterile 50 mL conical tube, combine 9 mL of the DMEM + 10% FBS base medium prepared in Step 1 with 1 mL of sterile tissue culture grade Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO). Mix gently by inverting the tube several times. Do not vortex vigorously, as DMSO can generate heat. This will create a 10% v/v DMSO solution in DMEM + 10% FBS.
steps.notes: DMSO is hygroscopic; ensure the bottle is tightly sealed when not in use. It is also toxic; wear appropriate personal protective equipment.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, sterile 10% DMSO in DMEM + 10% FBS.
troubleshooting: If solution appears cloudy: Discard and re-prepare. Could indicate contamination or degradation.
If too much heat is generated: Mixing too vigorously. Mix slowly and gently.

steps.title: Prepare 0.5 M Trehalose Stock Solution
steps.body_md: Weigh out 1.802 g of D-(+)-Trehalose dihydrate and transfer it to a sterile 100 mL graduated cylinder. Add sterile distilled water up to the 10 mL mark. Mix thoroughly by inversion or gentle swirling until the trehalose is fully dissolved. Filter sterilize the solution using a 0.22 µm syringe filter into a sterile tube.
steps.notes: The molecular weight of trehalose dihydrate is 360.32 g/mol. For a 0.5 M solution, 0.5 mol/L * 360.32 g/mol * 0.01 L = 1.8016 g. Round to 1.802 g for practicality.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, sterile 0.5 M trehalose stock solution.

steps.title: Prepare Trehalose Cryoprotectant Medium
steps.body_md: In a sterile 50 mL conical tube, combine 9 mL of the DMEM + 10% FBS base medium prepared in Step 1 with 1 mL of the 0.5 M Trehalose stock solution prepared in Step 3. Mix gently by inverting the tube several times. This will result in a 0.05 M trehalose solution (50 mM trehalose) in DMEM + 10% FBS.
steps.notes: The final trehalose concentration in the cryoprotectant medium will be 0.05 M (50 mM) as indicated in the hypothesis, due to the 1:10 dilution of the 0.5 M trehalose stock.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, sterile 0.05 M trehalose in DMEM + 10% FBS.

steps.title: Prepare 0.5 M Sucrose Stock Solution
steps.body_md: Weigh out 1.712 g of D-(+)-Sucrose and transfer it to a sterile 100 mL graduated cylinder. Add sterile distilled water up to the 10 mL mark. Mix thoroughly by inversion or gentle swirling until the sucrose is fully dissolved. Filter sterilize the solution using a 0.22 µm syringe filter into a sterile tube.
steps.notes: The molecular weight of sucrose is 342.30 g/mol. For a 0.5 M solution, 0.5 mol/L * 342.30 g/mol * 0.01 L = 1.7115 g. Round to 1.712 g for practicality.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, sterile 0.5 M sucrose stock solution.

steps.title: Prepare Sucrose Cryoprotectant Medium
steps.body_md: In a sterile 50 mL conical tube, combine 9 mL of the DMEM + 10% FBS base medium prepared in Step 1 with 1 mL of the 0.5 M Sucrose stock solution prepared in Step 5. Mix gently by inverting the tube several times. This will result in a 0.05 M sucrose solution (50 mM sucrose) in DMEM + 10% FBS.
steps.notes: The final sucrose concentration in the cryoprotectant medium will be 0.05 M (50 mM) as indicated in the hypothesis, due to the 1:10 dilution of the 0.5 M sucrose stock.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, sterile 0.05 M sucrose in DMEM + 10% FBS.

steps.title: Label and Store Cryoprotectant Media
steps.body_md: Clearly label each tube with the cryoprotectant type (DMSO, Trehalose, Sucrose), concentration, date of preparation, and your initials. Store all prepared cryoprotectant media at 4 °C until ready to use. Use within 1 week of preparation to ensure sterility and stability.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Three distinct, clearly labeled tubes containing the specified cryoprotectant solutions, stored at 4 °C.

success_criteria.how_measured: Confirmation of calculations and accurate weighing/pipetting
success_criteria.threshold: DMSO: 10% v/v; Trehalose: 0.05 M; Sucrose: 0.05 M (all in DMEM + 10% FBS)

success_criteria.how_measured: Visual inspection for turbidity; optional aliquot test on agar plate.
success_criteria.threshold: Solutions remain clear and free of microbial contamination after 24-48h at 37°C if tested.

procedures.name: Cell Cryopreservation
procedures.intent: To freeze HeLa cells using specified slow-rate freezing protocols with each of the prepared cryoprotectant media.

steps.title: Prepare HeLa Cells for Cryopreservation
steps.body_md: Harvest actively growing HeLa cells from culture flasks according to standard lab protocols. Ensure cells are healthy and in log phase growth, typically 70-90% confluent. Count cells using an automated cell counter or hemocytometer to determine cell density. Adjust the cell suspension to achieve a concentration of `1.5 x 10^7 cells/mL` in serum-free growth medium (e.g., DMEM without FBS). This high concentration is a precursor to dilution with cryoprotectant media.
steps.notes: Ensure cell viability is >90% before proceeding to cryopreservation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A concentrated suspension of viable HeLa cells ready for cryoprotectant addition.
troubleshooting: If viability is low: Discard cells and start with a fresh culture. Cryopreservation of unhealthy cells will yield poor results.
If cell counts are inaccurate: Repeat counting in duplicate or triplicate for better precision.

steps.title: Centrifuge Cells to Pellet
steps.body_md: Transfer the adjusted cell suspension into appropriate centrifuge tubes (e.g., 15 mL Falcon tube) and centrifuge to pellet cells. After centrifugation, carefully aspirate the supernatant, leaving approximately 0.5 mL of residual medium with the cell pellet.
steps.notes: The centrifugation speed and time are optimized to pellet mammalian cells gently without causing excessive stress.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A visible, compact cell pellet at the bottom of the tube.
troubleshooting: If no visible pellet: Resuspend and re-centrifuge at a slightly higher speed or longer duration. Check initial cell concentration.

steps.title: Resuspend Cell Pellet in Control Freezing Medium (DMSO based)
steps.body_md: Gently resuspend one of the cell pellets in `0.67 mL` of the pre-chilled 'Control Freezing Medium (DMSO based)'. Pipette gently up and down a few times, taking care not to introduce bubbles, to ensure an even, single-cell suspension. Ensure the entire pellet is dispersed.
steps.notes: This will result in a final volume of ~1 mL cryopreserved cells at `1x10^6 cells/mL` post-dilution when added to a 0.33 mL aliquot from Step 1.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A homogeneous, milky cell suspension without clumps.
troubleshooting: If clumps are present: Continue gentle pipetting. Avoid vigorous mixing that can damage cell membranes. Filter through a cell strainer if aggregation persists.

reagent_recipes.name: Control Freezing Medium (DMSO based)
reagent_recipes.notes: Sterile filter after mixing. Store at 4°C. Pre-chill before use.

steps.title: Resuspend Cell Pellet in Trehalose-DMSO Freezing Medium
steps.body_md: In parallel with Step 3, gently resuspend another cell pellet in `0.67 mL` of the pre-chilled 'Trehalose-DMSO Freezing Medium'. Pipette gently up and down a few times, taking care not to introduce bubbles, to ensure an even, single-cell suspension. Ensure the entire pellet is dispersed.
steps.notes: This cryoprotectant combines Trehalose and DMSO which will be compared to the Control Freezing Medium.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A homogeneous, milky cell suspension without clumps.
troubleshooting: If clumps are present: Continue gentle pipetting. Avoid vigorous mixing that can damage cell membranes. Filter through a cell strainer if aggregation persists.

reagent_recipes.name: Trehalose-DMSO Freezing Medium
reagent_recipes.notes: Dissolve trehalose in DMEM, then add DMSO. Adjust volume to 50 mL with serum-free DMEM. Sterile filter after mixing. Pre-chill before use. Final concentrations: 0.5M Trehalose, 10% (v/v) DMSO.

steps.title: Resuspend Cell Pellet in Sucrose-DMSO Freezing Medium
steps.body_md: In parallel with Steps 3 and 4, gently resuspend another cell pellet in `0.67 mL` of the pre-chilled 'Sucrose-DMSO Freezing Medium'. Pipette gently up and down a few times, taking care not to introduce bubbles, to ensure an even, single-cell suspension. Ensure the entire pellet is dispersed.
steps.notes: This medium uses sucrose as a cryoprotectant in combination with DMSO, serving as an additional comparison point. The source protocol used M9 buffer for freezing medium, but for mammalian cells, serum-free growth media is more appropriate.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A homogeneous, milky cell suspension without clumps.
troubleshooting: If clumps are present: Continue gentle pipetting. Avoid vigorous mixing that can damage cell membranes. Filter through a cell strainer if aggregation persists.

reagent_recipes.name: Sucrose-DMSO Freezing Medium
reagent_recipes.notes: Dissolve sucrose in DMEM, then add DMSO. Adjust volume to 50 mL with serum-free DMEM. Sterile filter after mixing. Pre-chill before use. Final concentrations: 0.5M Sucrose, 10% (v/v) DMSO.

steps.title: Aliquot Cell Suspensions into Cryovials
steps.body_md: Immediately after resuspension in each freezing medium (Control, Trehalose, Sucrose), aliquot `1 mL` of the cell suspension into pre-labeled `1.5 mL` sterile cryovials. Cap the cryovials securely.
steps.notes: Work swiftly. The time from adding cryoprotectant to initiating slow freezing should not exceed 15 minutes to maximize cell viability.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryovials filled with labeled cell suspensions.
troubleshooting: If significant time passes: Discard vials and repeat with fresh cells if possible. Extended exposure to cryoprotectants at room temperature can be toxic.

steps.title: Initiate Slow Freezing in a Controlled Rate Device
steps.body_md: Place the capped cryovials into a controlled-rate freezing device (e.g., Mr. Frosty container filled with isopropanol or a programmable cell freezer). Ensure the cryovials are positioned correctly for even cooling. Place the device into a `-80 °C` freezer.
equipment_needed: Mr. Frosty freezing container or programmable cell freezer
steps.notes: Mr. Frosty provides a cooling rate of approximately 1°C per minute, which is suitable for slow-rate freezing of most mammalian cells. A programmable freezer offers more precise control.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryovials slowly freezing within the programmed range.
troubleshooting: If thawing observed: Ensure -80°C freezer is functioning correctly and placed in a stable position.

steps.title: Transfer to Liquid Nitrogen Storage
steps.body_md: After `24 hours` of slow freezing at `-80 °C`, carefully transfer the cryovials to a liquid nitrogen dewar for long-term storage. Ensure proper safety precautions are followed when handling liquid nitrogen.
steps.notes: Storing cells in liquid nitrogen (-196 °C) ensures long-term viability by halting metabolic activity.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryovials securely stored in liquid nitrogen.

equipment: Mr. Frosty freezing container or programmable cell freezer

deviations_from_source.from_source: The source protocol `260183` is for `C. elegans` cryopreservation.
deviations_from_source.to_adapted: This protocol has been adapted for `HeLa cells`, a mammalian cell line. This required changes in cell handling, freezing media compositions, and specific cryopreservation parameters.
deviations_from_source.reason: The experimental hypothesis specifically targets `HeLa cells`, necessitating a complete adaptation of the source protocol, which was designed for `C. elegans` worms.

deviations_from_source.from_source: Source protocol `260183` uses M9 buffer for washes and as a base for the freezing buffer (TDMSO).
deviations_from_source.to_adapted: This adapted protocol uses standard mammalian cell media (e.g., DMEM) for initial cell handling and as a base for the freezing medium components (Trehalose-DMSO Freezing Medium, Sucrose-DMSO Freezing Medium). For the control, FBS is used with DMSO.
deviations_from_source.reason: M9 buffer is a minimal medium for `C. elegans` and is not suitable for preserving mammalian cell viability. Mammalian cells require more complex media formulations.

deviations_from_source.from_source: Source protocol `260183` specifies cell numbers suitable for `C. elegans`.
deviations_from_source.to_adapted: The key parameter `cell_density_for_freezing` has been set to `1x10^6 cells/mL` in the final cryovial for `HeLa cells`.
deviations_from_source.reason: Optimal freezing density for mammalian cell lines like HeLa is typically around `10^6 - 10^7 cells/mL` to ensure good post-thaw viability. The exact concentration in the key parameters `1x10^6 cells/mL` is a common and effective density for `HeLa` cells.

deviations_from_source.from_source: Source protocol `260183` lists TDMSO (Trehalose 0.08M, DMSO 0.5M) for cryopreservation.
deviations_from_source.to_adapted: This adapted protocol includes three distinct freezing media: 1) 'Control Freezing Medium (DMSO based)' (90% FBS, 10% DMSO), 2) 'Trehalose-DMSO Freezing Medium' (0.5M Trehalose, 10% DMSO in serum-free DMEM), and 3) 'Sucrose-DMSO Freezing Medium' (0.5M Sucrose, 10% DMSO in serum-free DMEM). The concentrations of trehalose and DMSO are adjusted as per the hypothesis, and sucrose is introduced as an additional comparison.
deviations_from_source.reason: The hypothesis specifically investigates the effect of trehalose versus sucrose cryoprotection against a standard DMSO-based protocol for `HeLa cells`. Therefore, specific freezing media formulations, concentrations, and an additional intervention (sucrose) are necessary.

deviations_from_source.from_source: Source protocol `260183` suggests placing cryovials at `-1 °C` for `10-15 minutes` before transferring to `-80 °C`.
deviations_from_source.to_adapted: This adapted protocol uses a controlled slow-freezing method (e.g., Mr. Frosty or programmable freezer) to directly cool vials to `-80 °C` over approximately 24 hours.
deviations_from_source.reason: For mammalian cell cryopreservation, a controlled-rate freezing process (typically `1°C/minute`) down to `-80 °C` is standard practice and more effective than a brief incubation at `-1 °C` to minimize ice crystal formation and osmotic shock.

success_criteria.how_measured: Count a small aliquot before freezing
Visual inspection by microscope after resuspension in freezing medium

success_criteria.what: Time from cryoprotectant addition to slow freezing initiation

procedures.name: Long-Term Storage
procedures.intent: To transfer and store the cryopreserved HeLa cells in liquid nitrogen for the duration of the experiment.

steps.title: Prepare liquid nitrogen storage Dewar
steps.body_md: Ensure the liquid nitrogen storage Dewar is clean, dry, and filled to an appropriate level with liquid nitrogen. Verify that internal racks are properly installed and labeled.
steps.notes: Optimal fill level typically allows for vapor phase storage of cryoboxes without frequent refilling.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Dewar is ready for cryovial insertion.
troubleshooting: If Dewar is not clean: Clean and dry thoroughly to prevent contamination.
If liquid nitrogen level is low: Refill carefully using appropriate safety measures.

steps.title: Organize cryovials in cryoboxes
steps.body_md: Place the cryovials containing the cryopreserved HeLa cells into labeled cryoboxes. Ensure that each cryobox is clearly identified with the experiment name, date, and cell type, and that individual cryovial positions within the box are recorded for easy retrieval.
steps.notes: Use a lab-specific inventory system/spreadsheet to record the exact location of each cryovial (e.g., Dewar ID, rack ID, box ID, row/column).
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryovials are securely organized and labeled.

steps.title: Transfer cryoboxes to liquid nitrogen Dewar
steps.body_md: Wearing appropriate cryogenic personal protective equipment (PPE) including cryo-gloves and a face shield, carefully transfer the cryoboxes into the designated racks within the liquid nitrogen storage Dewar. Minimize the time the Dewar lid is open to prevent excessive liquid nitrogen evaporation and condensation.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm whether storage should be in liquid phase (submerged) or vapor phase (above liquid level but within cryogenic temperatures).
steps.notes: The choice between liquid phase and vapor phase storage depends on institutional safety guidelines and potential risks of pathogen cross-contamination. Vapor phase is generally preferred for safety and reduced contamination risk.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryoboxes are safely placed within the Dewar, preserving the cells at cryogenic temperatures.
troubleshooting: If cryovials warm up during transfer: Immediately return to LN2 or dispose if warming was significant. Risk of cell damage.
If Dewar lid is open for too long: Replenish LN2 more frequently and monitor temperature carefully to avoid temperature fluctuations for stored samples.

steps.title: Secure and monitor Dewar
steps.body_md: Close the liquid nitrogen Dewar lid securely. Implement a regular monitoring schedule (e.g., weekly) to check the liquid nitrogen level and record it in the lab logbook. Refill liquid nitrogen as necessary to maintain the desired storage temperature.
equipment_needed: Liquid nitrogen dip stick or level sensor
steps.notes: Consistent monitoring ensures sample integrity. The frequency of refilling depends on the Dewar's capacity and usage.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryovials are maintained at cryogenic temperatures suitable for long-term storage.
troubleshooting: If LN2 level drops rapidly: Inspect Dewar for leaks or excessive usage. Repair or replace as needed.
If Dewar consistently runs low: Adjust refill schedule or consider a larger capacity Dewar.

equipment: Liquid nitrogen dip stick or level sensor

success_criteria.threshold: All cryovials are correctly stored in the designated liquid nitrogen Dewar according to the lab's inventory system.
Liquid nitrogen level is consistently maintained above the critical level to ensure all samples are kept at cryogenic temperatures (typically within the vapor phase or submerged).

procedures.name: Post-Thaw Cell Culture
procedures.intent: To rapidly thaw cryopreserved HeLa cells and recover them in culture for 24 hours.

steps.title: Prepare Water Bath and Culture Medium
steps.body_md: Fill a water bath with sterile water and set its temperature to 37 °C. Warm an appropriate volume of pre-warmed recovery medium (DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% Penicillin/Streptomycin) in said water bath. Ensure the medium reaches 37 °C before use.
steps.notes: Ensuring the medium is at the correct temperature prevents thermal shock to the cells during thawing and dilution.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Water bath and recovery medium ready at 37 °C.

steps.title: Retrieve Cryovial
steps.body_md: Working quickly to minimize warming, retrieve a single cryovial of frozen HeLa cells from the liquid nitrogen storage. Ensure proper personal protective equipment (PPE) is worn, including cryogenic gloves and a face shield.
steps.notes: Exposure to room temperature for even short periods can reduce cell viability.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryovial promptly transferred from liquid nitrogen to immediate thawing.
troubleshooting: If vial warms significantly before thawing: Discard vial and retrieve another. Pre-thawing can cause ice crystal damage.

steps.title: Rapid Thaw of Cryovial
steps.body_md: Immediately immerse the retrieved cryovial in the 37 °C water bath. Agitate the vial gently by hand until only a small ice crystal remains (approx. 1-2 minutes). Do not allow the contents to fully thaw and then sit at 37 °C for an extended period.
steps.notes: Rapid thawing minimizes the formation of damaging ice crystals and the exposure time to cryoprotectants at warmer temperatures.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryovial contents mostly liquid, with a pea-sized ice crystal remaining.
troubleshooting: If vial fully thaws and sits in water bath: Proceed with caution, but be aware of potential viability loss due to cryoprotectant toxicity. Prolonged exposure to DMSO is cytotoxic.

steps.title: Transfer Cells to Conical Tube
steps.body_md: After thawing, immediately disinfect the outside of the cryovial with 70% ethanol. Transfer the entire contents of the cryovial to a sterile 15 mL conical tube.
steps.notes: Disinfection prevents contamination upon opening the vial in the hood.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cell suspension transferred cleanly to a sterile conical tube.

steps.title: Slow Dilution of Cryoprotectant
steps.body_md: Slowly add 1 mL of pre-warmed recovery medium (DMEM + 10% FBS + 1% Pen/Strep) dropwise to the cell suspension in the conical tube, mixing gently by inverting the tube a few times after each addition. Repeat this dilution step with another 1 mL of medium, for a total of 2 mL added.
steps.notes: Slow dilution helps prevent osmotic shock to the cells by gradually reducing the concentration of the cryoprotectant.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cell suspension gradually diluted without visible clumping.
troubleshooting: If cells clump: Ensure gentle mixing and dropwise addition of medium.

steps.title: Centrifuge Cells
steps.body_md: Centrifuge the cell suspension at 200 x g for 5 minutes at room temperature to pellet the cells.
steps.notes: This step removes the cryoprotectant and spent medium, which can be toxic if left in contact with cells for too long.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Visible cell pellet at the bottom of the conical tube (size approx. 1-2 mm in diameter).
troubleshooting: If no pellet visible or pellet is loose: Increase centrifugation time by 2-3 minutes, or slightly increase speed to 300 x g, ensuring it is not too harsh for HeLa cells.

steps.title: Aspirate Supernatant
steps.body_md: Carefully aspirate the supernatant, ensuring not to disturb the cell pellet. Leave a small residual volume (~50-100 μL) to avoid drying out the pellet.
steps.notes: Gentle aspiration is key to retaining maximum cell yield.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Supernatant removed, pellet intact.
troubleshooting: If cell pellet is accidentally aspirated: Re-centrifuge the aspirated supernatant if possible, or accept loss for this sample.

steps.title: Resuspend and Culture Cells
steps.body_md: Resuspend the cell pellet in 10 mL of fresh, pre-warmed recovery medium. Transfer the cell suspension to a T-75 tissue culture flask. Place the flask in a humidified incubator set to 37 °C with 5% CO2 for a 24-hour incubation period.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cells are adhering and beginning to spread, with minimal debris visible, after 24 hours.
troubleshooting: If cells do not adhere or show significant clumping: Check incubator conditions (CO2, temperature, humidity) and ensure medium pH is correct. Consider increasing FBS percentage to 15-20% for initial recovery.

success_criteria.what: Thawed cells successfully cultured for 24 hours
success_criteria.how_measured: Microscopic observation of cell adherence and spreading in the T-75 flask
success_criteria.threshold: At least 50% of the field of view populated with adherent, healthy-looking HeLa cells (not rounded or detached), with minimal cell debris

procedures.name: Viability Measurement by Trypan Blue Exclusion
procedures.intent: To quantify the percentage of viable cells 24 hours post-thaw using trypan blue exclusion assay.

steps.title: Prepare Trypan Blue Solution
steps.body_md: If not already prepared, dilute a stock solution of 0.4% w/v Trypan Blue in 1X PBS to ensure a working concentration of 0.4%. Ensure the solution is sterile-filtered and stored at room temperature away from light.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Ready-to-use 0.4% Trypan Blue solution.

steps.title: Harvest HeLa Cells
steps.body_md: Gently detach HeLa cells from their culture vessel 24 hours post-thaw according to standard cell culture procedures (e.g., trypsinization). Neutralize trypsin activity with complete growth medium (DMEM with 10% FBS) and collect the cell suspension into a sterile centrifuge tube.
steps.notes: Ensure gentle handling to minimize cell damage.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A uniform suspension of HeLa cells.
troubleshooting: If cells are clumpy: Gently pipette the suspension up and down to dissociate clumps.
If cell yield is low: Ensure complete detachment of cells from the vessel surface.

steps.title: Centrifuge and Resuspend Cells
steps.body_md: Centrifuge the cell suspension at 300 x g for 5 minutes at room temperature to pellet the cells. Carefully aspirate the supernatant, ensuring not to disturb the cell pellet. Resuspend the cell pellet in 1X PBS to achieve a final concentration of approximately 1 x 10^5 to 1 x 10^6 cells/mL.
steps.notes: The exact volume of PBS for resuspension will depend on the estimated cell yield.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A single, well-defined cell pellet that is easily resuspended.
troubleshooting: If pellet is loose or poorly formed: Check centrifuge speed and duration. Ensure no significant residue remains from the supernatant.

steps.title: Mix Cells with Trypan Blue
steps.body_md: In a clean microcentrifuge tube, mix 10 uL of the cell suspension with 10 uL of 0.4% Trypan Blue solution. Gently pipette up and down several times to ensure thorough mixing.
steps.notes: Maintain a 1:1 ratio of cell suspension to Trypan Blue for accurate assessment.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A uniformly mixed, light blue solution.
troubleshooting: If mixture is not uniform: Pipette gently for a few more seconds. Avoid creating bubbles.

steps.title: Incubate for Staining
steps.body_md: Incubate the cell-Trypan Blue mixture at room temperature for 2-5 minutes. Do not exceed 5 minutes, as prolonged exposure can lead to uptake of dye by viable cells.
steps.notes: Optimal incubation time ensures sufficient dye uptake by compromised cells without affecting viable cells.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear differentiation between viable (unstained) and non-viable (blue) cells.
troubleshooting: If all cells are stained blue: Incubation time may be too long, or cell membrane integrity is severely compromised (e.g., dead cells).
If no cells are stained: Check Trypan Blue concentration and ensure mixing was adequate. It could also indicate extremely high viability.

steps.title: Load Hemocytometer (Manual Counting)
steps.body_md: Carefully load 10 uL of the stained cell mixture onto each side of a hemocytometer, ensuring no air bubbles are trapped.
steps.notes: Alternatively, if using an automated cell counter, follow the manufacturer's instructions for sample loading.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Hemocytometer chambers evenly filled with cell suspension, free of bubbles.
troubleshooting: If bubbles are present: Clean hemocytometer and coverslip thoroughly and reload. Ensure pipette tip is fully immersed before dispensing.
If chambers are under/over-filled: Adjust loading volume and technique.

steps.title: Count Cells
steps.body_md: Using a brightfield microscope at 10x or 20x magnification, count the number of viable (unstained, bright) and non-viable (blue, dark) cells in the four large corner squares of the hemocytometer. Count at least 100-200 cells per sample to ensure statistical significance.
steps.notes: For automated cell counters, input the values as per manufacturer's software. Ensure the counting area is clearly defined.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Distinct counts for viable and non-viable cells.
troubleshooting: If cells are too dense/sparse for counting: Adjust initial cell suspension concentration and re-prepare the sample.
If distinguishing viable/non-viable is difficult: Adjust microscope focus and illumination.

steps.title: Calculate Viability and Total Cell Concentration
steps.body_md: Calculate the percentage viability using the formula: (Number of viable cells / Total number of cells) * 100%. Also calculate the total cell concentration (cells/mL) using the formula: (Average total cells per square * Dilution factor * 10^4). The dilution factor for a 1:1 mixture with Trypan Blue is 2.
todo_for_researcher: Ensure correct dilution factor is used if a different cell:trypan blue ratio was used.
steps.notes: Record all counts and calculations meticulously for downstream analysis.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Percentage viability and total cell concentration for each sample.

success_criteria.what: Clear distinction between viable and non-viable cells under the microscope.
success_criteria.threshold: Viable cells appear bright and refractile, non-viable cells are stained blue.

success_criteria.what: Consistent cell counts across replicate squares/measurements.
success_criteria.how_measured: Comparing cell counts between hemocytometer squares or replicate automated counts.
success_criteria.threshold: Replicate counts should be within 10% of each other.

success_criteria.what: Final calculated viability percentage and cell concentration.
success_criteria.threshold: A quantifiable percentage of viable cells and total cell concentration for each experimental condition.

steps.title: Retrieve and Warm Culture Medium
steps.body_md: Retrieve the complete HeLa cell culture medium (DMEM supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and 1% Penicillin/Streptomycin) from cold storage. Allow the medium to warm to room temperature (18-25°C) or place it in a 37°C water bath for approximately 30 minutes to reach recommended culture temperature.
steps.notes: Ensure the medium is completely thawed and warmed to physiological temperature before use to prevent thermal shock to the cells.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Culture medium warmed to 37°C.

steps.title: Aspirate Spent Medium
steps.body_md: Transfer the HeLa cell culture flask (e.g., T-75 flask) into a Biological Safety Cabinet (BSC). Aspirate the spent culture medium from the flask using a sterile serological pipette attached to a vacuum pump. Tilt the flask gently to ensure complete removal of medium without disturbing the cell monolayer.
steps.notes: Work aseptically at all times to prevent contamination.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Flask free of spent medium, cells adhered to surface.
troubleshooting: If cells detach during aspiration: Reduce aspiration speed and ensure the pipette tip does not directly contact the cell monolayer.

steps.title: Wash Cells with PBS
steps.body_md: Add 5-10 mL of sterile 1x Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS) without Ca2+ or Mg2+ to the flask. Gently rock the flask back and forth 2-3 times to wash the cell monolayer and remove any residual serum, which can inhibit trypsin activity. Aspirate the PBS completely.
steps.notes: Complete removal of serum is crucial for effective trypsinization.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cells washed free of residual medium and serum.

steps.title: Trypsinize Cells
steps.body_md: Add 2-3 mL of 0.25% Trypsin-EDTA solution (w/v) to the flask. Ensure the entire cell monolayer is covered. Incubate the flask at 37°C in a CO2 incubator for 3-5 minutes. Periodically check under a microscope for cell detachment. Tap the side of the flask gently if cells are slow to detach.
steps.notes: Over-trypsinization can damage cells. Under-trypsinization leads to poor cell yield. Optimize incubation time for your specific cell line and conditions.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cells appear rounded and detached from the flask surface.
troubleshooting: If cells aren't detaching: Continue incubation for another 1-2 minutes, then gently tap the flask. Avoid scraping.
If cells look granular/damaged: Reduce trypsin incubation time or concentration for future passages.

steps.title: Neutralize Trypsin and Collect Cells
steps.body_md: Once cells are detached, add 5 mL of complete HeLa cell culture medium (from Step 1) to the flask to neutralize the trypsin. Pipette the cell suspension up and down several times to dissociate cell clumps and create a single-cell suspension. Transfer the entire cell suspension to a sterile 15 mL conical tube.
steps.notes: FBS in the complete medium contains inhibitors that will neutralize trypsin.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Homogeneous single-cell suspension in a conical tube.
troubleshooting: If excessive cell clumping: Gently pipette solution more vigorously, or consider adding a small amount of DNase I to subsequent trypsinization steps if cell death is suspected.

steps.title: Centrifuge Cells
steps.body_md: Centrifuge the 15 mL conical tube containing the cell suspension at 300 x g for 5 minutes at room temperature (20-25°C).
steps.notes: Ensure the centrifuge is balanced before spinning.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A compact, visible cell pellet at the bottom of the tube.
troubleshooting: If no visible pellet: Increase centrifugation speed or duration, ensure enough cells were transferred.

steps.title: Resuspend Cells and Count
steps.body_md: Aspirate the supernatant carefully, ensuring not to disturb the cell pellet. Resuspend the cell pellet in an appropriate volume (e.g., 1-2 mL) of fresh complete HeLa cell culture medium. Gently pipette up and down to achieve a uniform single-cell suspension. Take a 10 µL aliquot and dilute 1:1 with 0.4% Trypan Blue solution. Load 10 µL of the stained cell suspension onto a hemocytometer and count viable and non-viable cells.
steps.notes: Accurate cell counting is essential for calculating seeding density and viability. Viable cells exclude trypan blue and appear bright, while non-viable cells stain blue.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cell count and viability percentage determined.
troubleshooting: If cell counts are inconsistent: Ensure proper mixing of cell suspension before taking aliquots, and clean hemocytometer thoroughly.
If viability is low (<90%): Assess health of parent culture, ensure proper trypsinization, and consider discarding the culture if viability is consistently poor.

steps.title: Adjust Cell Density for Cryopreservation
steps.body_md: Based on the cell count from Step 7, adjust the cell suspension to the required density for cryopreservation, which is typically 1 x 10^6 to 5 x 10^6 cells/mL for HeLa cells. Use complete HeLa cell culture medium to dilute the cells if necessary, or centrifuge and resuspend in a smaller volume if too dilute.
steps.notes: The exact cell density will depend on the downstream cryopreservation protocol and desired recovery.
steps.anticipated_outcome: HeLa cell suspension at the target density for cryopreservation.

steps.title: Prepare DMEM + 10% FBS Base Medium (Control)
steps.body_md: Aseptically prepare urn:nil:uuid:5ca8dc4c-22ca-4c07-b2fb-ec55099fe682 of Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) containing 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) in a sterile beaker with a stir bar. This will serve as the base medium for all cryoprotectant preparations and as the control freezing medium.
steps.notes: Ensure FBS is heat-inactivated if not purchased as such, to prevent complement-mediated cell lysis.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, sterile DMEM + 10% FBS solution.

steps.title: Prepare DMSO Cryoprotectant Medium
steps.body_md: In a sterile 50 mL conical tube, combine 9 mL of the DMEM + 10% FBS base medium prepared in Step 1 with 1 mL of sterile tissue culture grade Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO). Mix gently by inverting the tube several times. Do not vortex vigorously, as DMSO can generate heat. This will create a 10% v/v DMSO solution in DMEM + 10% FBS.
steps.notes: DMSO is hygroscopic; ensure the bottle is tightly sealed when not in use. It is also toxic; wear appropriate personal protective equipment.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, sterile 10% DMSO in DMEM + 10% FBS.
troubleshooting: If solution appears cloudy: Discard and re-prepare. Could indicate contamination or degradation.
If too much heat is generated: Mixing too vigorously. Mix slowly and gently.

steps.title: Prepare 0.5 M Trehalose Stock Solution
steps.body_md: Weigh out 1.802 g of D-(+)-Trehalose dihydrate and transfer it to a sterile 100 mL graduated cylinder. Add sterile distilled water up to the 10 mL mark. Mix thoroughly by inversion or gentle swirling until the trehalose is fully dissolved. Filter sterilize the solution using a 0.22 µm syringe filter into a sterile tube.
steps.notes: The molecular weight of trehalose dihydrate is 360.32 g/mol. For a 0.5 M solution, 0.5 mol/L * 360.32 g/mol * 0.01 L = 1.8016 g. Round to 1.802 g for practicality.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, sterile 0.5 M trehalose stock solution.

steps.title: Prepare Trehalose Cryoprotectant Medium
steps.body_md: In a sterile 50 mL conical tube, combine 9 mL of the DMEM + 10% FBS base medium prepared in Step 1 with 1 mL of the 0.5 M Trehalose stock solution prepared in Step 3. Mix gently by inverting the tube several times. This will result in a 0.05 M trehalose solution (50 mM trehalose) in DMEM + 10% FBS.
steps.notes: The final trehalose concentration in the cryoprotectant medium will be 0.05 M (50 mM) as indicated in the hypothesis, due to the 1:10 dilution of the 0.5 M trehalose stock.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, sterile 0.05 M trehalose in DMEM + 10% FBS.

steps.title: Prepare 0.5 M Sucrose Stock Solution
steps.body_md: Weigh out 1.712 g of D-(+)-Sucrose and transfer it to a sterile 100 mL graduated cylinder. Add sterile distilled water up to the 10 mL mark. Mix thoroughly by inversion or gentle swirling until the sucrose is fully dissolved. Filter sterilize the solution using a 0.22 µm syringe filter into a sterile tube.
steps.notes: The molecular weight of sucrose is 342.30 g/mol. For a 0.5 M solution, 0.5 mol/L * 342.30 g/mol * 0.01 L = 1.7115 g. Round to 1.712 g for practicality.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, sterile 0.5 M sucrose stock solution.

steps.title: Prepare Sucrose Cryoprotectant Medium
steps.body_md: In a sterile 50 mL conical tube, combine 9 mL of the DMEM + 10% FBS base medium prepared in Step 1 with 1 mL of the 0.5 M Sucrose stock solution prepared in Step 5. Mix gently by inverting the tube several times. This will result in a 0.05 M sucrose solution (50 mM sucrose) in DMEM + 10% FBS.
steps.notes: The final sucrose concentration in the cryoprotectant medium will be 0.05 M (50 mM) as indicated in the hypothesis, due to the 1:10 dilution of the 0.5 M sucrose stock.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, sterile 0.05 M sucrose in DMEM + 10% FBS.

steps.title: Label and Store Cryoprotectant Media
steps.body_md: Clearly label each tube with the cryoprotectant type (DMSO, Trehalose, Sucrose), concentration, date of preparation, and your initials. Store all prepared cryoprotectant media at 4 °C until ready to use. Use within 1 week of preparation to ensure sterility and stability.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Three distinct, clearly labeled tubes containing the specified cryoprotectant solutions, stored at 4 °C.

steps.title: Prepare HeLa Cells for Cryopreservation
steps.body_md: Harvest actively growing HeLa cells from culture flasks according to standard lab protocols. Ensure cells are healthy and in log phase growth, typically 70-90% confluent. Count cells using an automated cell counter or hemocytometer to determine cell density. Adjust the cell suspension to achieve a concentration of `1.5 x 10^7 cells/mL` in serum-free growth medium (e.g., DMEM without FBS). This high concentration is a precursor to dilution with cryoprotectant media.
steps.notes: Ensure cell viability is >90% before proceeding to cryopreservation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A concentrated suspension of viable HeLa cells ready for cryoprotectant addition.
troubleshooting: If viability is low: Discard cells and start with a fresh culture. Cryopreservation of unhealthy cells will yield poor results.
If cell counts are inaccurate: Repeat counting in duplicate or triplicate for better precision.

steps.title: Centrifuge Cells to Pellet
steps.body_md: Transfer the adjusted cell suspension into appropriate centrifuge tubes (e.g., 15 mL Falcon tube) and centrifuge to pellet cells. After centrifugation, carefully aspirate the supernatant, leaving approximately 0.5 mL of residual medium with the cell pellet.
steps.notes: The centrifugation speed and time are optimized to pellet mammalian cells gently without causing excessive stress.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A visible, compact cell pellet at the bottom of the tube.
troubleshooting: If no visible pellet: Resuspend and re-centrifuge at a slightly higher speed or longer duration. Check initial cell concentration.

steps.title: Resuspend Cell Pellet in Control Freezing Medium (DMSO based)
steps.body_md: Gently resuspend one of the cell pellets in `0.67 mL` of the pre-chilled 'Control Freezing Medium (DMSO based)'. Pipette gently up and down a few times, taking care not to introduce bubbles, to ensure an even, single-cell suspension. Ensure the entire pellet is dispersed.
steps.notes: This will result in a final volume of ~1 mL cryopreserved cells at `1x10^6 cells/mL` post-dilution when added to a 0.33 mL aliquot from Step 1.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A homogeneous, milky cell suspension without clumps.
troubleshooting: If clumps are present: Continue gentle pipetting. Avoid vigorous mixing that can damage cell membranes. Filter through a cell strainer if aggregation persists.

reagent_recipes.name: Control Freezing Medium (DMSO based)
reagent_recipes.notes: Sterile filter after mixing. Store at 4°C. Pre-chill before use.

steps.title: Resuspend Cell Pellet in Trehalose-DMSO Freezing Medium
steps.body_md: In parallel with Step 3, gently resuspend another cell pellet in `0.67 mL` of the pre-chilled 'Trehalose-DMSO Freezing Medium'. Pipette gently up and down a few times, taking care not to introduce bubbles, to ensure an even, single-cell suspension. Ensure the entire pellet is dispersed.
steps.notes: This cryoprotectant combines Trehalose and DMSO which will be compared to the Control Freezing Medium.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A homogeneous, milky cell suspension without clumps.
troubleshooting: If clumps are present: Continue gentle pipetting. Avoid vigorous mixing that can damage cell membranes. Filter through a cell strainer if aggregation persists.

reagent_recipes.name: Trehalose-DMSO Freezing Medium
reagent_recipes.notes: Dissolve trehalose in DMEM, then add DMSO. Adjust volume to 50 mL with serum-free DMEM. Sterile filter after mixing. Pre-chill before use. Final concentrations: 0.5M Trehalose, 10% (v/v) DMSO.

steps.title: Resuspend Cell Pellet in Sucrose-DMSO Freezing Medium
steps.body_md: In parallel with Steps 3 and 4, gently resuspend another cell pellet in `0.67 mL` of the pre-chilled 'Sucrose-DMSO Freezing Medium'. Pipette gently up and down a few times, taking care not to introduce bubbles, to ensure an even, single-cell suspension. Ensure the entire pellet is dispersed.
steps.notes: This medium uses sucrose as a cryoprotectant in combination with DMSO, serving as an additional comparison point. The source protocol used M9 buffer for freezing medium, but for mammalian cells, serum-free growth media is more appropriate.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A homogeneous, milky cell suspension without clumps.
troubleshooting: If clumps are present: Continue gentle pipetting. Avoid vigorous mixing that can damage cell membranes. Filter through a cell strainer if aggregation persists.

reagent_recipes.name: Sucrose-DMSO Freezing Medium
reagent_recipes.notes: Dissolve sucrose in DMEM, then add DMSO. Adjust volume to 50 mL with serum-free DMEM. Sterile filter after mixing. Pre-chill before use. Final concentrations: 0.5M Sucrose, 10% (v/v) DMSO.

steps.title: Aliquot Cell Suspensions into Cryovials
steps.body_md: Immediately after resuspension in each freezing medium (Control, Trehalose, Sucrose), aliquot `1 mL` of the cell suspension into pre-labeled `1.5 mL` sterile cryovials. Cap the cryovials securely.
steps.notes: Work swiftly. The time from adding cryoprotectant to initiating slow freezing should not exceed 15 minutes to maximize cell viability.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryovials filled with labeled cell suspensions.
troubleshooting: If significant time passes: Discard vials and repeat with fresh cells if possible. Extended exposure to cryoprotectants at room temperature can be toxic.

steps.title: Initiate Slow Freezing in a Controlled Rate Device
steps.body_md: Place the capped cryovials into a controlled-rate freezing device (e.g., Mr. Frosty container filled with isopropanol or a programmable cell freezer). Ensure the cryovials are positioned correctly for even cooling. Place the device into a `-80 °C` freezer.
equipment_needed: Mr. Frosty freezing container or programmable cell freezer
steps.notes: Mr. Frosty provides a cooling rate of approximately 1°C per minute, which is suitable for slow-rate freezing of most mammalian cells. A programmable freezer offers more precise control.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryovials slowly freezing within the programmed range.
troubleshooting: If thawing observed: Ensure -80°C freezer is functioning correctly and placed in a stable position.

steps.title: Transfer to Liquid Nitrogen Storage
steps.body_md: After `24 hours` of slow freezing at `-80 °C`, carefully transfer the cryovials to a liquid nitrogen dewar for long-term storage. Ensure proper safety precautions are followed when handling liquid nitrogen.
steps.notes: Storing cells in liquid nitrogen (-196 °C) ensures long-term viability by halting metabolic activity.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryovials securely stored in liquid nitrogen.

steps.title: Prepare liquid nitrogen storage Dewar
steps.body_md: Ensure the liquid nitrogen storage Dewar is clean, dry, and filled to an appropriate level with liquid nitrogen. Verify that internal racks are properly installed and labeled.
steps.notes: Optimal fill level typically allows for vapor phase storage of cryoboxes without frequent refilling.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Dewar is ready for cryovial insertion.
troubleshooting: If Dewar is not clean: Clean and dry thoroughly to prevent contamination.
If liquid nitrogen level is low: Refill carefully using appropriate safety measures.

steps.title: Organize cryovials in cryoboxes
steps.body_md: Place the cryovials containing the cryopreserved HeLa cells into labeled cryoboxes. Ensure that each cryobox is clearly identified with the experiment name, date, and cell type, and that individual cryovial positions within the box are recorded for easy retrieval.
steps.notes: Use a lab-specific inventory system/spreadsheet to record the exact location of each cryovial (e.g., Dewar ID, rack ID, box ID, row/column).
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryovials are securely organized and labeled.

steps.title: Transfer cryoboxes to liquid nitrogen Dewar
steps.body_md: Wearing appropriate cryogenic personal protective equipment (PPE) including cryo-gloves and a face shield, carefully transfer the cryoboxes into the designated racks within the liquid nitrogen storage Dewar. Minimize the time the Dewar lid is open to prevent excessive liquid nitrogen evaporation and condensation.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm whether storage should be in liquid phase (submerged) or vapor phase (above liquid level but within cryogenic temperatures).
steps.notes: The choice between liquid phase and vapor phase storage depends on institutional safety guidelines and potential risks of pathogen cross-contamination. Vapor phase is generally preferred for safety and reduced contamination risk.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryoboxes are safely placed within the Dewar, preserving the cells at cryogenic temperatures.
troubleshooting: If cryovials warm up during transfer: Immediately return to LN2 or dispose if warming was significant. Risk of cell damage.
If Dewar lid is open for too long: Replenish LN2 more frequently and monitor temperature carefully to avoid temperature fluctuations for stored samples.

steps.title: Secure and monitor Dewar
steps.body_md: Close the liquid nitrogen Dewar lid securely. Implement a regular monitoring schedule (e.g., weekly) to check the liquid nitrogen level and record it in the lab logbook. Refill liquid nitrogen as necessary to maintain the desired storage temperature.
equipment_needed: Liquid nitrogen dip stick or level sensor
steps.notes: Consistent monitoring ensures sample integrity. The frequency of refilling depends on the Dewar's capacity and usage.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryovials are maintained at cryogenic temperatures suitable for long-term storage.
troubleshooting: If LN2 level drops rapidly: Inspect Dewar for leaks or excessive usage. Repair or replace as needed.
If Dewar consistently runs low: Adjust refill schedule or consider a larger capacity Dewar.

steps.title: Prepare Water Bath and Culture Medium
steps.body_md: Fill a water bath with sterile water and set its temperature to 37 °C. Warm an appropriate volume of pre-warmed recovery medium (DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% Penicillin/Streptomycin) in said water bath. Ensure the medium reaches 37 °C before use.
steps.notes: Ensuring the medium is at the correct temperature prevents thermal shock to the cells during thawing and dilution.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Water bath and recovery medium ready at 37 °C.

steps.title: Retrieve Cryovial
steps.body_md: Working quickly to minimize warming, retrieve a single cryovial of frozen HeLa cells from the liquid nitrogen storage. Ensure proper personal protective equipment (PPE) is worn, including cryogenic gloves and a face shield.
steps.notes: Exposure to room temperature for even short periods can reduce cell viability.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryovial promptly transferred from liquid nitrogen to immediate thawing.
troubleshooting: If vial warms significantly before thawing: Discard vial and retrieve another. Pre-thawing can cause ice crystal damage.

steps.title: Rapid Thaw of Cryovial
steps.body_md: Immediately immerse the retrieved cryovial in the 37 °C water bath. Agitate the vial gently by hand until only a small ice crystal remains (approx. 1-2 minutes). Do not allow the contents to fully thaw and then sit at 37 °C for an extended period.
steps.notes: Rapid thawing minimizes the formation of damaging ice crystals and the exposure time to cryoprotectants at warmer temperatures.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cryovial contents mostly liquid, with a pea-sized ice crystal remaining.
troubleshooting: If vial fully thaws and sits in water bath: Proceed with caution, but be aware of potential viability loss due to cryoprotectant toxicity. Prolonged exposure to DMSO is cytotoxic.

steps.title: Transfer Cells to Conical Tube
steps.body_md: After thawing, immediately disinfect the outside of the cryovial with 70% ethanol. Transfer the entire contents of the cryovial to a sterile 15 mL conical tube.
steps.notes: Disinfection prevents contamination upon opening the vial in the hood.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cell suspension transferred cleanly to a sterile conical tube.

steps.title: Slow Dilution of Cryoprotectant
steps.body_md: Slowly add 1 mL of pre-warmed recovery medium (DMEM + 10% FBS + 1% Pen/Strep) dropwise to the cell suspension in the conical tube, mixing gently by inverting the tube a few times after each addition. Repeat this dilution step with another 1 mL of medium, for a total of 2 mL added.
steps.notes: Slow dilution helps prevent osmotic shock to the cells by gradually reducing the concentration of the cryoprotectant.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cell suspension gradually diluted without visible clumping.
troubleshooting: If cells clump: Ensure gentle mixing and dropwise addition of medium.

steps.title: Centrifuge Cells
steps.body_md: Centrifuge the cell suspension at 200 x g for 5 minutes at room temperature to pellet the cells.
steps.notes: This step removes the cryoprotectant and spent medium, which can be toxic if left in contact with cells for too long.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Visible cell pellet at the bottom of the conical tube (size approx. 1-2 mm in diameter).
troubleshooting: If no pellet visible or pellet is loose: Increase centrifugation time by 2-3 minutes, or slightly increase speed to 300 x g, ensuring it is not too harsh for HeLa cells.

steps.title: Aspirate Supernatant
steps.body_md: Carefully aspirate the supernatant, ensuring not to disturb the cell pellet. Leave a small residual volume (~50-100 μL) to avoid drying out the pellet.
steps.notes: Gentle aspiration is key to retaining maximum cell yield.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Supernatant removed, pellet intact.
troubleshooting: If cell pellet is accidentally aspirated: Re-centrifuge the aspirated supernatant if possible, or accept loss for this sample.

steps.title: Resuspend and Culture Cells
steps.body_md: Resuspend the cell pellet in 10 mL of fresh, pre-warmed recovery medium. Transfer the cell suspension to a T-75 tissue culture flask. Place the flask in a humidified incubator set to 37 °C with 5% CO2 for a 24-hour incubation period.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Cells are adhering and beginning to spread, with minimal debris visible, after 24 hours.
troubleshooting: If cells do not adhere or show significant clumping: Check incubator conditions (CO2, temperature, humidity) and ensure medium pH is correct. Consider increasing FBS percentage to 15-20% for initial recovery.

steps.title: Prepare Trypan Blue Solution
steps.body_md: If not already prepared, dilute a stock solution of 0.4% w/v Trypan Blue in 1X PBS to ensure a working concentration of 0.4%. Ensure the solution is sterile-filtered and stored at room temperature away from light.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Ready-to-use 0.4% Trypan Blue solution.

steps.title: Harvest HeLa Cells
steps.body_md: Gently detach HeLa cells from their culture vessel 24 hours post-thaw according to standard cell culture procedures (e.g., trypsinization). Neutralize trypsin activity with complete growth medium (DMEM with 10% FBS) and collect the cell suspension into a sterile centrifuge tube.
steps.notes: Ensure gentle handling to minimize cell damage.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A uniform suspension of HeLa cells.
troubleshooting: If cells are clumpy: Gently pipette the suspension up and down to dissociate clumps.
If cell yield is low: Ensure complete detachment of cells from the vessel surface.

steps.title: Centrifuge and Resuspend Cells
steps.body_md: Centrifuge the cell suspension at 300 x g for 5 minutes at room temperature to pellet the cells. Carefully aspirate the supernatant, ensuring not to disturb the cell pellet. Resuspend the cell pellet in 1X PBS to achieve a final concentration of approximately 1 x 10^5 to 1 x 10^6 cells/mL.
steps.notes: The exact volume of PBS for resuspension will depend on the estimated cell yield.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A single, well-defined cell pellet that is easily resuspended.
troubleshooting: If pellet is loose or poorly formed: Check centrifuge speed and duration. Ensure no significant residue remains from the supernatant.

steps.title: Mix Cells with Trypan Blue
steps.body_md: In a clean microcentrifuge tube, mix 10 uL of the cell suspension with 10 uL of 0.4% Trypan Blue solution. Gently pipette up and down several times to ensure thorough mixing.
steps.notes: Maintain a 1:1 ratio of cell suspension to Trypan Blue for accurate assessment.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A uniformly mixed, light blue solution.
troubleshooting: If mixture is not uniform: Pipette gently for a few more seconds. Avoid creating bubbles.

steps.title: Incubate for Staining
steps.body_md: Incubate the cell-Trypan Blue mixture at room temperature for 2-5 minutes. Do not exceed 5 minutes, as prolonged exposure can lead to uptake of dye by viable cells.
steps.notes: Optimal incubation time ensures sufficient dye uptake by compromised cells without affecting viable cells.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear differentiation between viable (unstained) and non-viable (blue) cells.
troubleshooting: If all cells are stained blue: Incubation time may be too long, or cell membrane integrity is severely compromised (e.g., dead cells).
If no cells are stained: Check Trypan Blue concentration and ensure mixing was adequate. It could also indicate extremely high viability.

steps.title: Load Hemocytometer (Manual Counting)
steps.body_md: Carefully load 10 uL of the stained cell mixture onto each side of a hemocytometer, ensuring no air bubbles are trapped.
steps.notes: Alternatively, if using an automated cell counter, follow the manufacturer's instructions for sample loading.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Hemocytometer chambers evenly filled with cell suspension, free of bubbles.
troubleshooting: If bubbles are present: Clean hemocytometer and coverslip thoroughly and reload. Ensure pipette tip is fully immersed before dispensing.
If chambers are under/over-filled: Adjust loading volume and technique.

steps.title: Count Cells
steps.body_md: Using a brightfield microscope at 10x or 20x magnification, count the number of viable (unstained, bright) and non-viable (blue, dark) cells in the four large corner squares of the hemocytometer. Count at least 100-200 cells per sample to ensure statistical significance.
steps.notes: For automated cell counters, input the values as per manufacturer's software. Ensure the counting area is clearly defined.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Distinct counts for viable and non-viable cells.
troubleshooting: If cells are too dense/sparse for counting: Adjust initial cell suspension concentration and re-prepare the sample.
If distinguishing viable/non-viable is difficult: Adjust microscope focus and illumination.

steps.title: Calculate Viability and Total Cell Concentration
steps.body_md: Calculate the percentage viability using the formula: (Number of viable cells / Total number of cells) * 100%. Also calculate the total cell concentration (cells/mL) using the formula: (Average total cells per square * Dilution factor * 10^4). The dilution factor for a 1:1 mixture with Trypan Blue is 2.
todo_for_researcher: Ensure correct dilution factor is used if a different cell:trypan blue ratio was used.
steps.notes: Record all counts and calculations meticulously for downstream analysis.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Percentage viability and total cell concentration for each sample.
